5 min read
1 hour ago

--

Every C# developer knows List<T> and Dictionary<TKey, TValue>.
They’re fast, reliable, and have been the go-to collections for years.
But while reviewing an enterprise API recently, I noticed something interesting.
The application loaded thousands of configuration values during startup — country codes, feature flags, permission names, MIME types, and routing metadata. Once loaded, these values never changed. Yet every request still relied on a regular Dictionary<TKey, TValue>.
That made me wonder:
Why are we paying for a mutable collection when the data is completely immutable?
The answer led me to one of the most overlooked performance features introduced in .NET 8 — Frozen Collections.
Most experienced C# developers have heard about Span<T>, source generators, and Native AOT.
Surprisingly, many still haven’t discovered FrozenDictionary<TKey, TValue> and FrozenSet<T>.
If your application loads data once and performs millions of lookups afterward, these collections deserve your attention.
The Hidden Cost of Using Dictionary Everywhere
Dictionary<TKey, TValue> is one of the best data structures in .NET.
